hook: The world's only museum devoted entirely to penises
facts: Founded 1997 by retired headmaster Sigurdur Hjartarson
Began with a bull pizzle given as a staff-room joke
Holds 300-plus specimens from over 100 mammal species
Moved to its current Hafnartorg space by the harbour in 2020
w: What started as a single bull pizzle, handed to a headmaster as a staff-room gag, snowballed into 300-plus specimens from whale to field mouse, plus the long-promised human donation that finally arrived. Sigurdur Hjartarson spent decades collecting. Earnest, deadpan, and entirely unembarrassed.

hook: Iceland's punk story told inside a former public toilet
facts: Opened 2016 in disused underground toilets on Bankastraeti
The lavatory operated 1930 to 2000, nicknamed Nullid, 'the Zero'
Grand opening hosted by Sex Pistols' John Lydon
Exhibits crammed into old cubicles and washbasins
w: Down a stairwell into the old gents on Bankastraeti, where locals once relieved themselves at the address they called 'the Zero'. Posters, handbills and headphone-fed bootlegs now fill the cubicles, charting the first fifteen years of Icelandic punk. John Lydon cut the ribbon. Snarl in the urinals.

hook: The shallow downtown pond locals call the biggest bread soup in the world
facts: Barely 80 cm at its deepest, kept ice-free in one corner by warm water
City Hall rises straight out of its northwest edge
Over forty bird species recorded; the geothermal patch keeps them through winter
Hljomskalagardur park lines its southern shore
legend: Locals say elves and hidden folk gather in the parks around its banks
Tales tell of a serpent in the water and a drowned woman's ghost on the ice
w: A pond so shallow you could wade most of it, yet the heart of the city wraps around it. They say elves keep house in the bankside parks, and that a serpent and a drowned woman haunt the water. One warm corner stays unfrozen all winter, packed with squabbling birds. Locals nicknamed it the world's largest bread soup.

hook: A tidal island lighthouse you can only reach when the sea allows
facts: Light first built 1897, rebuilt 1947, still operational
A sandspit submerges at high tide, stranding the unwary for hours
Nature reserve since 1974; closed during nesting, May to mid-July
Sits at the westernmost tip of the Reykjavik area
legend: Local lore carries tales of sea monsters and shipwrecks along this coast
w: The spit out to the lighthouse vanishes under the tide, and every year someone misreads the clock and waits, cold, for the sea to drop again. Birds rule the reserve in summer, so the gate stays shut. In winter it draws aurora-watchers to the dark western tip. Check the tide table or get marooned.

hook: A Viking-age longhouse preserved in situ beneath a downtown building
facts: Hall remains excavated in 2001 under Adalstraeti
A turf wall fragment predates the 871-plus-or-minus-2 tephra layer
Dating pinned by volcanic ash cross-matched to Greenland ice cores
Displayed exactly where it was dug, below street level
w: Descend below a downtown block and the oldest known wall in Reykjavik runs through the gloom, turf laid before a volcano dusted the whole island with ash in roughly 871. That ash, matched to Greenland's ice, gives the place its name. The longhouse stays exactly where the diggers found it. Settlement under the pavement.

hook: The white villa where Reagan and Gorbachev helped end the Cold War
facts: Built 1909 for the French consul to Iceland
Hosted the 1986 Reykjavik Summit; 'failed' talks that thawed the Cold War
Once the British Embassy residence
Stands alone on a lawn facing Faxafloi bay
legend: Staff spoke of a 'White Lady' ghost said to have spooked a British ambassador into selling up
w: Two superpowers sat in this modest seaside villa in 1986 and walked out without a deal, yet the Cold War never quite recovered its chill. Decades earlier a British ambassador, rattled by a 'White Lady' the staff swore roamed the halls, begged London to sell. Geothermal vents creak the timbers. History, with a haunting attached.

hook: Reykjavik's overgrown old graveyard, with a guardian of the dead
facts: Opened 1838 on Sudurgata, replacing a Viking-era burial ground
Tradition makes the first buried, Gudrun Oddsdottir, its protector
Independence hero Jon Sigurdsson and painter Kjarval lie here
Effectively full by the 1930s; a leafy maze of leaning stones
legend: Icelandic custom holds the first person buried becomes the cemetery's guardian spirit
w: Custom says the first soul buried in any Icelandic graveyard becomes its guardian, and Gudrun Oddsdottir, lowered in 1838, took the post here. Independence heroes and the country's great painter lie beneath trees planted as living memorials, now a green tangle of moss and leaning crosses. The largest museum in the city, some call it.

hook: Brooding mythic bronzes in a free garden behind a fortress-like museum
facts: Garden opened 1984 behind Iceland's first sculptor's studio-home
Twenty-six bronze casts of Einar Jonsson's works
Sits in the shadow of Hallgrimskirkja, open day and night, free
Jonsson lived 1874 to 1954, steeped in Norse myth and mysticism
w: Behind a windowless white block that looks more bunker than gallery, twenty-six bronzes wrestle with trolls, angels and the weight of Norse myth. Einar Jonsson, Iceland's first sculptor, lived and worked here and willed his strange visions to the nation. The garden never closes and costs nothing. Mysticism cast in metal.

hook: Iceland's weekend bazaar of woollens, junk and fermented shark
facts: Running since 1989, in the old Customs House since 1994
Name means 'the coal yard', after the site's earlier trade
Food side sells dried fish, skyr and cube-cut hakarl
Tryggvagata 19, beside the famous hot-dog stand
w: Weekends only, the old Customs House fills with trestle tables of secondhand books, lopapeysa sweaters and the kind of clutter you dig for. One wall over, vendors hand out toothpicked cubes of hakarl, the cured shark that flattens first-timers. Named for the coal that once piled here. Bring cash and a strong stomach.

hook: A Cod War coast guard cutter you can board by the old harbour
facts: Set in a former fish-freezing plant on Grandagardur
Centrepiece: the 1959 coast guard vessel Odinn
Odinn fought in all three Cod Wars against British trawlers
Its net-cutting trawl warp blade still sits on the afterdeck
w: Tied up outside an old freezing plant sits Odinn, the grey cutter that rammed and slashed British trawler nets through three Cod Wars in the cold standoff over fishing grounds. Climb aboard and stand on the afterdeck where the warp-cutting blade did its work. Inside, the saga of a nation that fed itself from the sea.

hook: Twenty-three life-size whales hanging in a darkened warehouse
facts: Models of every whale species in Icelandic waters
Centrepiece: a 25-metre blue whale, largest animal ever to live
Each model hand-painted from a real, identified individual
Housed in a warehouse near the old harbour
w: Walk into a blacked-out warehouse and twenty-three whales loom overhead at full scale, a 25-metre blue whale among them, each modelled on a real animal down to its scars. The lighting drops you to the seabed and the audio fills with their song. Soft to the touch, oddly tender. The ocean, brought indoors and hung from the ceiling.

hook: Yoko Ono's beam of light to John Lennon, lit on a ferry-only island
facts: Unveiled 2007 on Videy, on what would have been Lennon's 67th birthday
Fifteen searchlights throw a column of light from a stone wishing well
'Imagine Peace' carved into the base in 24 languages
Lit each year between October 9 and December 8
w: On an uninhabited island reached only by ferry, a stone well throws a tower of light miles into the polar sky each autumn, Yoko Ono's memorial to a husband shot in 1980. The base carries one wish in two dozen tongues. Powered, fittingly, by Iceland's own geothermal grid. A lighthouse for an idea.

hook: Reykjavik's oldest cafe, unchanged since the first espresso in 1958
facts: Opened 1958, first place in Iceland to serve espresso
Founder learned the craft studying art in Italy
Still run by the same family, decor barely touched
Doubles as a rotating gallery for local artists
w: An art student came home from Italy in 1958 and pulled the first espresso ever served in Iceland, and the machine has barely cooled since. The teak panelling, the waffles, the same family at the counter, all of it preserved like aspic. An Italian envoy once called it the best coffee north of the Alps. Time, stopped, with crema.

hook: A grass spiral mound topped by a fish-drying shed, by the harbour
facts: Built 2013, designed by artist Olof Nordal for a fish factory
An 8-metre turf-covered hill of some 4,500 tons of stone
A spiral path climbs to a traditional fish-drying hut on top
Stands on the Grandi waterfront among the fishing sheds
w: A commissioned grass tussock, eight metres of turf wound round a spiral path, rising out of the working harbour with a real fish-drying shed perched on its crown. Walk the spiral up and the whole city and bay swing into view. Olof Nordal built it as a place to pause. A hill that did not exist, doing the city a quiet favour.

hook: The city's oldest pool, a 1937 art deco temple to geothermal bathing
facts: Opened 1937, designed by Gudjon Samuelsson
Among the first buildings to use piped geothermal water
Same architect later designed Hallgrimskirkja up the hill
Narrow art deco windows; rooftop hot tubs added later
w: Reykjavik's first proper pool, opened 1937 in pale art deco lines by the architect who later raised Hallgrimskirkja on the hill above. It ran on some of the earliest geothermal water piped into town, and generations learned to swim under its tall windows. Soak in a rooftop tub with the church spire watching. Civic ritual, steaming.

hook: A sculptor's self-built white dome, half Reykjavik, half Cairo
facts: Sculptor Asmundur Sveinsson built it as home and studio, 1942 to 1959
Dome and pyramids drawn from Mediterranean and Middle Eastern forms
Now a branch of the Reykjavik Art Museum
Free garden of monumental sculpture, open day and night
w: A self-taught sculptor spent seventeen years building his own house, a whitewashed dome flanked by little pyramids, as if he had dragged Cairo and the Aegean to a subarctic suburb. Around it, monumental bronzes of working folk and folklore fill a garden that never shuts. Asmundur Sveinsson lived inside his own sculpture.

hook: The 1937 hot-dog stand where Bill Clinton ordered his with mustard
facts: Trading since 1937, an institution in a tiny red booth
Dogs are lamb-led, with pork and beef, in a soft bun
Bill Clinton's 2004 order birthed the 'Clinton', mustard only
Once named Europe's best hot-dog stand by The Guardian
w: A red plywood booth that has sold lamb-and-pork dogs since 1937, with the queue snaking down Tryggvagata whatever the weather. Order 'one with everything', crisp onion top and bottom, the local rite. In 2004 Bill Clinton, on a diet, took his with mustard alone, so that order now bears his name. Europe's best, by one paper's reckoning.

hook: Alvar Aalto's blue-roofed cultural house adrift in a marsh
facts: Designed by Finnish modernist Alvar Aalto, opened 1968
Built on a man-made rise in the Vatnsmyri wetland
Ultramarine ceramic roof echoes the distant mountains
Library holds 30,000 items in Nordic languages, but not Icelandic
w: Alvar Aalto set this low white building on a made-up island in a marsh south of downtown, capping it with a blue-glazed roofline tuned to the mountains beyond. Down in the sunken library, 30,000 books span every Nordic tongue except, pointedly, Icelandic. Birds wheel over the wetland outside. A Finn's quiet masterpiece, off most maps.

hook: The oldest house in the city, born of Iceland's first factories
facts: Built 1762, the oldest surviving house in Reykjavik
Raised by Skuli Magnusson as part of the Innrettingar workshops
Magnusson is remembered as the father of Reykjavik
Once held the country's first modern grocery, run by Silli and Valdi
w: When this timber house went up in 1762, Reykjavik amounted to a few farms, and Skuli Magnusson meant his row of wool workshops to drag the place into the modern age. They flopped, but the house stood, later a bishop's home and the city's first proper grocer's. Now part of the City Museum. Where the capital effectively began.

hook: The green-spired free church on the pond, a progressive outlier
facts: Congregation founded 1899, church consecrated 1903
Lutheran but independent of the state Church of Iceland
Distinctive green tower, square base turning octagonal above
Long active on women's suffrage and gay rights causes
w: White walls and a green tower reflected in the pond, raised in 1903 by people who broke from the state church not over doctrine but to bring it closer to the street. It has campaigned ever since for women's votes and gay rights, and its acoustics make it a favourite concert hall. The reformers' church, still facing the water.
